speaker: Guest
text_en: Basically Gmail, and then all the rest is in this tiny little part of the graph.
text: 基本上就是 Gmail，然后图上其他所有东西都挤在一个特别特别小的角落里。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So we thought that after using Gmail, I used it since I don't know when I signed up, a few weeks into it, I got one of those invite codes.
text: 所以我们就想，我用了 Gmail 之后——我都不记得自己是什么时候注册的了，反正用了没几周，我就拿到了那种邀请码。

speaker: Host
text_en: That was a really clever launch.
text: 那次发布真的很聪明。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I used it ever since.
text: 然后我从那以后就一直在用。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So that's literally 17 years or something of that, of Gmail usage.
text: 所以算下来，我用 Gmail 真的是用了 17 年左右。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And over that time I built up a lot of opinions about things that didn't work quite like I would prefer it to work.
text: 而在这段时间里，我对很多地方都慢慢形成了不少看法，因为它们的运作方式并不完全符合我的偏好。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And we put all those opinions into a new software product.
text: 于是我们把这些想法全都放进了一款新的软件产品里。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Spent about almost two years developing it, millions of dollars in accumulative R&D funds, and launched it in the summer of 2020, which, by the way, time to launch a product, well, 2020 wasn't great for a whole host of different reasons.
text: 我们花了差不多将近两年时间来开发它，累计投入了数百万美元的研发资金，并在 2020 年夏天发布了它。顺便说一句，要说发布产品的时机嘛，2020 年因为各种各样的原因，实在不算理想。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We were kind of trying to slot in a...
text: 我们当时算是想硬塞进一个……

speaker: Host
text_en: Can there just be a week where the whole world is not just insane?
text: 难道就不能有那么一周，整个世界别这么疯吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: We finally picked a week.
text: 我们总算挑中了一周。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We went live, and then we had the battle of our lives with Apple.
text: 我们上线了，然后就跟 Apple 打了一场这辈子最艰难的仗。

speaker: Host
text_en: With Apple.
text: 跟 Apple。

speaker: Host
text_en: I remember that.
text: 我记得那事。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And ultimately ended up...
text: 最后结果就变成了……

speaker: Host
text_en: They didn't want to approve your app.
text: 他们不想批准你们的 app。

speaker: Guest
text_en: They didn't want to approve our app unless we paid the toll fee, the 30%.
text: 除非我们交那笔过路费，也就是 30%，不然他们就不愿意批准我们的 app。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And they were basically willing to say, you can't be in the App Store, which for an email product like that is a death sentence.
text: 而且他们基本上就是在说，你不能上 App Store，这对那样一款邮件产品来说简直是判了死刑。

speaker: Host
text_en: Yes.
text: 对。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You have to be on not just mobile phones, but specifically the iPhone.
text: 你必须覆盖的不只是手机端，而且还得是 iPhone。

speaker: Host
text_en: This is true today.
text: 现在也是这样。

speaker: Guest
text_en: S.
text: 美国。

speaker: Guest
text_en: is the most affluent software market in the world.
text: 是全世界最富裕的软件市场。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So for that business to work, we needed to be on the iPhone.
text: 所以那门生意要想跑通，我们就必须上 iPhone。

speaker: Guest
text_en: After a two-week epic struggle back and forth, thankfully time to perfection with WWDC, where Apple preferably didn't want to look like the Goliath squashing a...
text: 经过两周来来回回、堪称史诗级的拉扯，好在时间点刚好卡在 WWDC，Apple 大概也不想让自己看起来像那个一脚踩扁……的 Goliath。

speaker: Host
text_en: Tiny David.
text: 小小的 David。

speaker: Guest
text_en: developer, a tiny developer.
text: 一个开发者，一个小小的开发者。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We ended up being allowed in, and Apple sort of rewrote the rules after the fact to make it fit.
text: 最后我们还是被允许进去了，而 Apple 事后某种程度上改写了规则，好让这件事说得通。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It was a small victory, not the ultimate victory, but at least it allowed us to be there.
text: 这算是一次小胜，不是最终的胜利，但至少让我们能待在那里。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And Hey ended up being an enormous success, in part, ironically, because Apple gave us wall-to-wall coverage for two weeks.
text: 而 Hey 最后取得了巨大的成功，讽刺的是，部分原因恰恰是 Apple 连续两周给了我们铺天盖地的曝光。

speaker: Guest
text_en: When I look back upon that, I think I wouldn't have gambled like that because the outcome would have been zero, right?
text: 现在回头看，我觉得我当时本来不会去冒那样的险，因为结果本来可能就是零，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like Apple refuses our app.
text: 比如 Apple 直接拒掉我们的 app。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We sign up 200 people, and the app is dead.
text: 我们拉来 200 个人注册，结果这个 app 就直接死机了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: What instead happened was they gave us a multi-million dollar launch campaign and coverage in all major media, and we signed up tens of thousands of people in those first weeks.
text: 但实际发生的是，他们给了我们一场价值数百万美元的上线推广活动，还拿到了所有主流媒体的报道，于是最开始那几周我们就有了几万人的注册。

speaker: Guest
text_en: That was an insane event, but also very satisfying.
text: 那真是个疯狂的时刻，不过也特别让人满足。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And the other satisfying thing was I just love Hey.
text: 还有另一件让我很满足的事，就是我真的很喜欢 Hey。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I use it every day.
text: 我每天都在用它。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I basically use Basecamp in terms of web applications.
text: 说到 web 应用，我基本上主要用的就是 Basecamp。

speaker: Guest
text_en: That's where we do all our collaborative work.
text: 我们所有协作性的工作都是在那上面完成的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then my number two app—and many days, it's my number one app—is Hey because I just do all my stuff in email.
text: 然后我的第二常用 app——很多时候其实是第一常用——就是 Hey，因为我基本上所有事情都是通过 email 处理的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I am constantly communicating with people.
text: 我一直都在和人沟通。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'm writing.
text: 我在写东西。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'm doing a lot of stuff in email, as many people do.
text: 我也会像很多人一样，在邮件里处理很多事情。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And having that be a pleasurable experience and a nice environment and my inbox being a little more sacred than what happens with Gmail where total strangers around the world can just make your pocket buzz if you have notifications turned on, which they are by default, just seems insane to me, right?
text: 所以，邮件这件事如果能变成一种愉快的体验，有个更舒服的环境，而且我的收件箱能更神圣一点，而不是像 Gmail 那样——只要你开着通知，而且默认就是开着的，世界上任何一个陌生人都能让你口袋里的手机震一下——这在我看来简直太离谱了，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: This idea that there's direct access to one of my most important daily priority lists.
text: 居然会有这种设定：别人可以直接触达我每天最重要的一份优先事项清单。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like anyone can put something on that?
text: 就好像谁都能往上面塞点东西？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Insane.
text: 太离谱了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Anyway, Hey doesn't do that.
text: 总之，Hey 不会这样。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We have the screener and no one gets to reach your inbox before you've said, I want to hear from this person.
text: 我们有个筛选机制，在你明确说“我想听这个人说什么”之前，谁都到不了你的收件箱。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And most of the time I say no to most people, right?
text: 而且大多数时候，我对大多数人都会说不，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like things end up in the screener and we have thumbs up.
text: 比如消息会先进筛选器，然后我们会给个通过。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I will hear from this person.
text: 这个人我会听。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Thumbs down.
text: 不通过。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'll never hear from that person again.
text: 那我就再也不会听到这个人的消息了。

speaker: Host
text_en: This is how I reached out.
text: 我当时就是这么联系到你的。

speaker: Host
text_en: I mean, we were, I'm not sure we were connected on X, but I said email because your email is out there and your screener seems to have worked because it gave me the thumbs up.
text: 我的意思是，我们当时好像不确定有没有在 X 上互相关注，但我发了邮件，因为你的邮箱是公开的，而且你的筛选机制看起来确实起作用了，因为它给我点了通过。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It did because the screener is me.
text: 有用，因为筛选的人就是我。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So there's not even AI trying to suss out whether I want to hear from you or not.
text: 所以甚至都没有 AI 来判断我到底想不想收到你的消息。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Because what turns out to be true is it's actually not that onerous to once a day, go through your screener and say thumbs up or down because there aren't that many people in the world.
text: 因为事实证明，其实每天花一次时间过一遍筛选列表，然后点个赞或踩，并没有那么麻烦，毕竟这世界上也没那么多人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And if you say no to the annoying pestering salespeople who within Gmail managed to read your inbox seven times, then the workload is much less.
text: 而且如果你把那些烦人的、死缠烂打的销售都拒掉——他们在 Gmail 里居然能把你的收件箱翻来覆去看七遍——那工作量就小多了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And it's very satisfying, I would say too, because when I was using Gmail, I would get roped into this sales tactic that they of course rely on, which is that like you write back and say like, no, thank you.
text: 而且我还想说，这种方式很有满足感，因为我以前用 Gmail 的时候，总会被他们惯用的销售话术套进去，就是你会回一句，像是“不用了，谢谢”。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'm not interested.
text: 我不感兴趣。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then they would respond again.
text: 然后他们还会再回你一次。

speaker: Host
text_en: Yes.
text: 对。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And now you feel like, wait, am I now obligated to respond to this person?
text: 然后你就会觉得，等等，我现在是不是有义务回复这个人了？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I kind of feel like I am.
text: 我多少会有这种感觉。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And occasionally I would end up writing.
text: 而且我有时候最后还真会回信。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And even if I wouldn't write, they still have access to my inbox.
text: 就算我不回，他们也还是能进到我的收件箱。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So I would hear from them again.
text: 所以他们还会再联系我。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Next week, they have a whole drip campaign.
text: 到了下一周，他们还有一整套滴灌式邮件营销。

speaker: Host
text_en: They all fucking do, right?
text: 他们他妈的全都这样，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: That any outreach is seven emails.
text: 基本上任何一次主动触达，都是七封邮件起步。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's not one emails.
text: 不是一封邮件。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's seven emails.
text: 是七封邮件。

speaker: Host
text_en: And if you show any sign of life, it's probably 52.
text: 而且你只要稍微露出一点活人的迹象，可能就会变成 52 封。

speaker: Guest
text_en: That's just not how it works in Hey.
text: 但在 Hey 里根本不是这么运作的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I say thumbs down one time, never hear from that person again.
text: 我只要点一次踩，以后就再也不会收到那个人的消息了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's actually amazing how quickly you can curate your garden from that.
text: 说真的，靠这个你能多快把自己的花园打理好，真的很惊人。
